rationale: The citing paper uses Jindal and Newberry (2018) as more than background: it relies on that paper's fixed bargaining-cost perspective to frame why the decision to initiate negotiation matters in hybrid pricing. In the results and discussion, it explicitly compares its IPD effect to the fixed-cost mechanism, noting that higher IPD similarly reduces negotiation initiation and then contrasting profit implications with the 2018 findings. This role meaningfully informs the citing paper's conceptual positioning and interpretation, even without direct method reuse.
relation_description: The citing paper extends the cited fixed-cost bargaining line by introducing advertised reference prices (via initial perceived discount) as a new driver of negotiation initiation, negotiated discounts, and revenue outcomes in hybrid pricing settings. It builds on the cited paper's bargaining-initiation mechanism to interpret how IPD changes behavior and profitability.
